annotations.title: Offered gifts.
annotations.text: Either God himself taught Adam, and he his children, or else they knew by instinct of nature, that Sacrifice must be offered to God, to acknowledge thereby his supreme dominion over man, and man's due subjection to his divine Majesty. And that not only in internal affection, which (as S. Augustin, [1] and all Catholic Doctors teach) is principally required, but also in external things, [2] because we consist of body, and not only of soul, and have, by God's goodness, the use of corporal things. As here we see example in the law of nature: and the same was ordained by written precept in the law of Moyses: [3] the Prophets also foretold, that external Sacrifice should be offered in the law of grace and new Testament, to wit, the same which Christ instituted, and left in his Church, to continue to the end of the world. Moreover this homage of offering Sacrifice is so peculiar to God only, [4] that albeit many other exterior rites and services are used both to God & men, as to be bare-head, to bow, to kneel, & the like before them, either of great humility (saith S. Augustin) [5] or of pestiferous flattery, to such as are homines colendi, venerandi, si autem eis multum additur, & adorandi: men to be worshipped, reverenced, and if much be given them, adored; (for this term of adoring is also applied to men in holy Scriptures, Gen. 23. v. 7. 17. v. 29) yet Sacrifice is due to God only, and to no creature how excellent so ever. In so much (saith the same doctor) that as all nations found it necessary to offer Sacrifice, so none durst sacrifice to any, nisi ei quem Deum aut scivit, aut putavit, aut finxit; but to him whom they either knew, or thought, or feigned to be God.

notes.text: Lib. 10. de civit. c. 5.
External Sacrifice due to God in every Law.
Levit. 1. Dan. 12. Mal. 1. Luc. 22.
Sacrifice due to God only and to no creature.
Lib. 10. civit. cap. 4. Aristot. li. 2. Metaphis. Ethic. 9. Polit. 7. c. 8.

annotations.title: Had respect to Abel.
annotations.text: [1] Both Cain and Abel did well in offering external Sacrifice, but they differed much in sincerity and manner of choosing or dividing their oblations, touching God's part and their own, as S. Justinus Martyr, S. Hierom, S. Augustin and others teach. [2] For Abel offered of the best things, of the first-begotten of his flock, and of their fat. And therefore God respected & approved it. But to Cain and to his gifts he had not respect, because he wanted sincere devotion. Which difference of God's acceptance appeared doubtless, as S. Hierom and S. Augustin supposed, by some external sign, otherwise Cain had not understood it. Most like it was by fire sent from God, which inflamed and consumed Abel's Sacrifice, & not Cain's. As we read of diverse other Sacrifices in holy Scriptures.

notes.text: Abel's Sacrifice declared acceptable, & not Cain's, by some external sign.
To. 3. q. 4. Quæst. Hebraic. Lib. 15. civit. c. 7. Mala. 1. Hebr. 11. Levit. 9. Iudic. 6. 2. Par. 7. 3. Reg. 18. 2. Mac. 1.

annotations.title: Shalt not thou receive.
annotations.text: [1] Reward of good works and punishment of evil are clearly proved by this place, God saying to Cain: If thou doest well, shalt thou not receive again? What else but well for well doing, as Abel received consolation of his Sacrifice well offered? But if thou doest ill, shall not thy sin be present forthwith at the door, afflicting thy conscience, and not suffering thy mind to be in quiet, for remorse of thy wicked fact, and fear of just judgement? For hence it came that Cain's countenance fell, and his stomach boiled with anger: punishment so beginning even in this life, & much more in the next world our Saviour will render (as himself saith) [2] to every man according to his works: which the Apostle expresseth more distinctly, eternal life, or wrath & indignation.

notes.text: Reward and punishment according to our works.
Mat. 16. Rom. 2.

annotations.title: under thee.
annotations.text: This Text so plainly sheweth freewill in man, [1] also after his fall, that the English Protestants to avoid so clear a truth, for these words, the lust thereof (to wit of sin) shall be under thee, and thou shalt have dominion over it, corruptly translate in some of their Bibles thus: [2] [3] Unto thee his desire shall be subject, and thou shalt rule over him. As if God had said, that Abel should be under Cain. As the phantastical Manichees perverted the sense, whose absurdity S. Augustin controlleth, [4] maintaining the true construction of the words, Tu dominaberis illius; nunquid fratris? absit. Cuius igitur, nisi peccati? Thou shalt rule over; what, over thy brother? Not so. Over what then, but sin? In other English Editions, namely in the last, which we suppose they will stand to, it is better, but yet obscure, thus, Unto thee shall be the desire thereof, and thou shalt have rule of it. Let us therefore examine the sense, and if S. Hierome, [5] the great scripture Doctor did rightly understand it, God did speak to this effect to Cain: Because thou hast freewill, I warn thee, that sin have not dominion over thee, but thou over sin. [6] The Hebrew hath thus: ad te appetitus eius, & tu dominaberis in eum, or, ei. Unto thee the appetite thereof, and thou shalt rule over it. Thargum Hierosolimitanum concludeth God's speech to Cain thus: Into thy hand I have given power of thy concupiscence, and have thou dominion thereof: whether thou wilt to good or to evil. The Greek hath thus: To thee is the conversion thereof, and thou shalt bear rule over it: to wit, appetite, lust, concupiscence is under thy will. Finally, [7] [8] all antiquity, universality, and uniform consent of Christian Doctors, and other learned Philosophers, and reasonable men hold it for certain and an evident truth, that man, yea a sinner, hath freewill. Yet Luther, the father of Protestants, [9] so abhorred this truth, that he could not abide the very word, nor vouchsafe (when he writ against it) to title his beastly book, Contra liberum arbitrium, Against freewill but, De servo arbitrio, Of servile arbitrament. And denieth that man is in any wise free to choose, to resolve, or determine, but in all things servile, tied, constrained, and compelled to what soever he doth, saith, or thinketh. Further, that man in all his actions is like to a hackney, that is, forced to go whither the rider will have him. And knowing the whole world against him, shameth not to confess, that he setteth them all at naught in respect of himself, concluding thus: I have not (saith he) conferred with any in this book, but I have affirmed, and I do affirm. Neither will I that any man judge hereof, but I counsel all to obey, or yield to my opinion. [10] [11] Calvin also for his part conspireth in this heresy with Luther, but more faintly, rather wisheth than imagineth that men be so mad as to fly from the name of freewill. I (Saith Calvin) [12] neither myself would use this word, and would wish others, if they ask me counsel, to abstain from it. But we will be bold to oppose S. Hierome's reason against Luther, Calvin, all Manichees, and others that deny freewill. God made us (saith he) [13] with freewill, neither are we drawn by necessity to virtues nor to vices; otherwise where is necessity, there is neither damnation nor crown. [14]

notes.text: Freewill in man also after his fall.
Heretical translation.
Bible 1579.
lib. 15. c. 7. civit.
Quæst Hebraic. in Gen.
The Hebrew also & Greek text prove freewill in Cain.
Freewill testified by antiquity, universality, and consent of learned & reasonable persons.
S. Aug. l. de vera Rel. c. 14.
Luther abhorred the name of freewill.
li. de servo arbitrio.
Calvin also misliketh the word freewill.
l. 2. adv. Iovinian.
Where is necessity there is neither reward nor punishment due.

annotations.title: Cain went forth.
annotations.text: It is a mark of Heretics to make breach, [1] and go forth of the Church. And commonly it cometh of envy. Some run into heresies and schisms (saith S. Cyprian) [2] when they envy Bishops, whilst one either complaineth that himself was not rather ordained, or disdaineth to suffer another above him. Hereupon he kicketh, hereupon he rebelleth. Envy moved Cain to kill his brother, because his own works were wicked and rejected: and his brother's just, [3] and esteemed. So going forth became obstinate, obdurate, and desperate in his sin, and being reprobate of God, began a wicked City, opposite to the City of God. Wherefore Moyses, as S. Augustin noteth, [4] intending to describe and shew the perpetual continuance of God's City, the true Church, from Adam, which he doth by the line of Seth to Noe, & so forward to his own time, would not omit to tell also the progeny of Cain, even to the flood, wherein all his offspring was finally drowned & destroyed, that the true City of God might appear more distinct, more conspicuous, and more renowned. And that indeed the same only (and not any broken and interrupted companies or conventicles) might be known to be the true Church of God.

notes.text: Going forth of the Church a mark of Heretics.
1. Ioan. 2. Tract. de Zelo & livore.
lib. de Pastore. c. 8. & c. 20.

annotations.title: I have slain.
annotations.text: <mn>[1]</mn> So hard and obscure is this place, that S. Hierom required by S. Damasus Pope to expound it, <mn>[2]</mn> dareth not affirm any one sense for certain, but proposing diverse which the text may seem to bear, wisheth the Pope who was also very learned) to examine all more at large: putting him in mind that Origen writ his twelfth and thirteenth books upon this only place. <mn>[3]</mn> The most probable exposition seemeth to be gathered out of the Hebrew's Tradition, that this Lamech of the issue of Cain (for there was another Lamech of Seth's progeny) much addicted to hunting, & his eyes decaying, used in that exercise the direction of a young man his nephew, the son of Tubalcain. Who seeing something move in the bushes, supposing it to be a wild beast, willed his grand-father to shoot at the same: which he did, & struck the mark with a deadly wound, & approaching to take the prey, found it to be old Cain. Whereupon sore amazed, afflicted, & moved with great passion, did so beat the young man for his ill direction, that he also died of the dry blows. After both which mishaps, & his passion at last calmed, Lamech lamenteth, as the text saith, that he had killed a man & stripling, to wit, the one with a wound, the other with dry blows, for which he feared sevenfold punishment more than Cain suffered for killing Abel. Nevertheless S. Hierom & other <mn>[4]</mn> Fathers think it probable, that Lamech killing the one of ignorance, the other in passion, was not so severely punished as he feared. <mn>[5]</mn> And so they understand the rest of this passage, that sevenfold vengeance was taken of Cain, by prolongation of his miserable life till his seventh generation, when one of his own issue slew him, & another of the same linage with him. And Lamech was punished seventy sevenfold when his seventy seven children (for so many he had, as Josephus writeth) & all their offspring perished in the flood. <mn>[6]</mn> Mystically by seventy seven may be signified, that the sin of mankind should be punished & expiated in Christ our Redeemer, who was born in the seventy seventh generation from Adam.

notes.text: Scripture hard.
Tom 3. ad 1. quest. Damasi.
A probable sense according to the Hebrews' Tradition.
S. Chris. ho. 20. in Gen.
Lib. 1. Antiq. ca. 2. Origen in Gen. Luc. 3.
Mystical sense.

annotations.title: Began to invocate.
annotations.text: [1] Seth was a most holy man, and so brought up his children, that they were called the sons of God, Gen. 6. Adam also and Eve were penitent, & became great Confessors, and are now Saints. And so it cannot be doubted but amongst other spiritual exercises they prayed & invocated God. And therefore that which is here said: He (to wit Enos) began or (as the Hebrew hath) then was begun, to invocate the name of our Lord, cannot be understood of private, [2] but of some public prayer of many meeting together, & observing some rites and set form in peculiar place dedicated to divine Service, the Church being now grown to a competent multitude; and that besides Sacrifice, which was also before, as appeareth both by Cain and Abel.

notes.text: Suidas vocabulo Seth. Joseph. l. 1. Anti. S. Aug. epist. 99. ad Evod.
Public prayer besides Sacrifice in the Church of God.
